description: Transmits credentials as a Base64-encoded username:password pair in the Authorization header. Must be used over HTTPS — Base64 is encoding, not encryption.
Uses a bearer token (commonly a JWT) issued by an authorization server. The client includes the token in every request. Standard scheme for OAuth 2.0 APIs.
An improvement over Basic auth — credentials are hashed (MD5 or SHA-256) with a server-provided nonce, preventing replay attacks. Rarely used in modern APIs.
header_format: Authorization: Digest username="...", realm="...", nonce="...", uri="...", response="..."
example: Authorization: Digest username="user", realm="example.com", nonce="abc", response="hash"

description: OAuth 1.0a — signs each request with HMAC using the consumer secret and access token secret. Complex but does not require HTTPS (signature protects integrity). Largely superseded by OAuth 2.0.
Non-standard but widely used. A static secret key sent as a header (commonly X-API-Key) or query parameter. Easy to implement but offers no built-in expiry or scope.
Requests are signed by hashing the payload + timestamp + secret key with HMAC-SHA256. Prevents tampering and replay attacks. Used by AWS (AWS Signature v4) and Stripe webhooks.
JSON Web Tokens are self-contained tokens with a header, payload, and signature. Typically used as Bearer tokens. Stateless — server validates without a database lookup.

Both client and server present X.509 certificates. Verifies both parties' identities at the TLS layer — no separate HTTP Authorization header needed. Used in zero-trust and service mesh architectures.
header_format: N/A — authentication occurs at TLS handshake
example: Client presents client.crt signed by trusted CA during TLS handshake

description: Windows NT LAN Manager — a Microsoft challenge-response authentication protocol. Used in Windows environments for intranet single sign-on. Multi-step handshake; rarely used outside Windows ecosystems.
Negotiates between Kerberos (preferred) and NTLM. Used for Windows integrated authentication / SSO in enterprise intranets. Kerberos provides strong mutual authentication with a trusted KDC.
